section.title: Shepherd the Flock
section.sectionRationale: As the letter closes, Peter turns to the care of the church under pressure: elders are to shepherd the flock humbly under the Chief Shepherd, the young are to submit, and all are to be clothed in humility, casting their cares on God and resisting the devil. It gathers the letter's themes of suffering, humility, and hope into a final charge and a great promise from the God of all grace.
section.thesis: Under the Chief Shepherd who will appear with an unfading crown, elders shepherd God's flock willingly and humbly, all clothe themselves in humility and cast their anxieties on the God who cares for them, and resisting the devil they trust that the God of all grace will Himself restore and establish them after a little suffering.
section.frameNote: Three movements: shepherd the flock under the Chief Shepherd, verses 1 to 4; humble yourselves and cast your cares on God, verses 5 to 7; and resist the devil, trusting the God of all grace, verses 8 to 11.

verses.text: As a fellow elder, a witness of Christ’s sufferings, and a partaker of the glory to be revealed, I appeal to the elders among you:
Be {{poimaino|shepherds}} of God’s flock that is among you, watching over them not out of compulsion, but willingly, as God would have you; not out of greed, but out of eagerness;
not lording it over those entrusted to you, but being examples to the flock.
And when the {{archipoimen|Chief Shepherd}} appears, you will receive the crown of glory that will never fade away.

Young men, in the same way, submit yourselves to your elders. And all of you, clothe yourselves with {{tapeinophrosyne|humility}} toward one another, because, “God opposes the proud, but gives grace to the humble.”
Humble yourselves, therefore, under God’s mighty hand, so that in due time He may exalt you.
Cast all your {{merimna|anxiety}} on Him, because He cares for you.

units.label: Resist the devil, trust the God of all grace
units.note: He will restore and establish you

verses.text: Be sober-minded and alert. Your {{antidikos|adversary}} the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour.
Resist him, standing firm in your faith and in the knowledge that your brothers throughout the world are undergoing the same kinds of suffering.

And after you have suffered for a little while, the {{theos_charitos|God of all grace}}, who has called you to His eternal glory in Christ, will Himself {{katartizo|restore}} you, secure you, strengthen you, and establish you.
To Him be the power forever and ever. Amen.

poimaino.gloss: To shepherd, to tend a flock, including feeding, guiding, and guarding. Elders are to shepherd God's flock, not their own, caring for the people as a shepherd cares for sheep. The word echoes Jesus' charge to Peter, feed My sheep, and frames pastoral leadership as tender, watchful care rather than rule.

archipoimen.gloss: The Chief Shepherd, the head over all under-shepherds. Christ is the Chief Shepherd who owns the flock and will appear to reward faithful elders with an unfading crown of glory. Every human shepherd serves under Him and is accountable to Him, tending sheep that finally belong to Christ, not to themselves.

tapeinophrosyne.gloss: Humility, lowliness of mind. Peter tells all believers to clothe themselves with it toward one another, like tying on a servant's apron. Since God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, humility is the garment of those who live under God's mighty hand and expect Him, in time, to exalt them.

merimna.gloss: Anxiety, worry, the cares that weigh on the soul. Believers are to cast all their anxiety on God, throwing the whole burden onto Him. The ground is His personal care: He cares for you. Humility and casting cares belong together, for it is pride that insists on carrying its own burdens.

antidikos.gloss: An adversary, an opponent at law, an enemy. The devil is the believer's adversary, prowling like a roaring lion to devour. Yet he is to be resisted by standing firm in faith, not feared, for the God of all grace will soon restore His people. The enemy is real but already doomed under Christ's power.

theos_charitos.gloss: The God of all grace, the source of every kind of grace. Peter names God this way as he promises restoration after suffering. The God who has called His people to eternal glory in Christ is overflowing in grace, and it is His grace, not their strength, that will carry them through and bring them home.

katartizo.gloss: To restore, mend, or make complete, as one sets a broken bone or repairs a net. After a little suffering the God of all grace will Himself restore His people. The word promises that God does not merely rescue but mends what suffering has strained, making His people whole, firm, and fit for His eternal glory.

basic.context: Peter, himself an elder and a witness of Christ's sufferings, charges the church's elders: shepherd God's flock, not because you must or for money, but willingly and eagerly, not bossing people around but leading by example. When the Chief Shepherd, Jesus, appears, you will get a crown of glory that never fades. Younger believers should submit to the elders, and everyone should put on humility, because God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble. So humble yourself under God's mighty hand, cast all your worries on Him, because He genuinely cares for you. Stay alert: the devil prowls like a lion. Resist him, standing firm in faith. And know this: after a little suffering, the God of all grace will Himself restore you and make you strong.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the letter's closing charge, and it ties together its main threads: suffering, humility, and hope. Leadership is defined as servant shepherding under Christ the Chief Shepherd, never as domineering. The promise of verse 10 is the letter's summit: after a little while of suffering, God Himself will restore, secure, strengthen, and establish His people. The suffering is real but brief; the glory is eternal.
basic.originalLanguages: Elders are to poimaino, shepherd, God's flock, awaiting the archipoimen, the Chief Shepherd. All are to put on tapeinophrosyne, humility, and cast their merimna, anxiety, on God who cares. The antidikos, the adversary, the devil, is to be resisted. The theos pases charitos, the God of all grace, will Himself katartizo, restore, His people.
basic.typology: The shepherd imagery runs from the Lord as Israel's Shepherd in Psalm 23 and Ezekiel 34 to Jesus the Good Shepherd, and now to elders as under-shepherds of the Chief Shepherd. The crown of glory answers the fading crowns of this world. And God's mighty hand recalls the hand that delivered Israel from Egypt, now lifting the humble in due time.
basic.christ: Christ is the Chief Shepherd, the owner of the flock and the coming Judge and rewarder of faithful shepherds. He is the one whose sufferings Peter witnessed and whose glory will be revealed. It is in Him that God has called His people to eternal glory. The whole passage is oriented to His appearing, when the unfading crown is given.
basic.god: God is the one who opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, whose mighty hand humbles and exalts in due time, and who cares personally for His people so that they can cast all their cares on Him. Above all He is the God of all grace who, after a little suffering, will Himself restore, secure, strengthen, and establish His own. He is gracious, caring, mighty, and faithful to the end.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 34 contrasts false shepherds with God the true Shepherd. John 21 records Jesus charging Peter to feed His sheep. Proverbs 3 and James 4 say God gives grace to the humble. And Psalm 55 says cast your burden on the Lord, and He will sustain you.
basic.oneStory: God has always shepherded His people, raising under-shepherds and finally coming Himself in Christ, the Good and Chief Shepherd. The suffering church is His flock, kept through a little while of affliction toward eternal glory. Humbling themselves, casting their cares on Him, and resisting the enemy, believers rest in the God of all grace, who will Himself restore them and bring them home, to whom belongs the power forever.

context.literary: The final section moves from a charge to elders, verses 1 to 4, to a call for humility addressed to the young and to all, verses 5 to 7, to a summons to vigilance and resistance, verses 8 to 9, and climaxes in the great promise and doxology of verses 10 to 11. Peter's self-description as fellow elder and witness of Christ's sufferings ties the charge to his own life, and the shepherd and crown imagery frames the whole.
context.historical: The early churches were led by pluralities of elders, and the temptations named, reluctance, greed, and domineering, were real dangers. The image of the prowling lion may reflect the intensifying threat facing the churches, whether spiritual or literal danger. The reminder that brothers throughout the world suffer the same things situates the readers within a worldwide, embattled family.
context.canonical: The passage is a cornerstone of the New Testament's teaching on pastoral ministry, defining leadership as humble shepherding under Christ. It draws the biblical shepherd theme to a head in the title Chief Shepherd, and its promise from the God of all grace, restoring His people after suffering, forms a fitting summary of the letter's message of hope through trial.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To charge elders to shepherd God's flock humbly and willingly under the Chief Shepherd, to call all believers to humility, dependent prayer, and vigilant resistance of the devil, and to seal the letter with the promise that the God of all grace will Himself restore and establish His suffering people for eternal glory.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The section is mostly prescriptive: shepherd, submit, clothe yourselves in humility, cast your cares, be alert, resist. But the commands rest on descriptions of God, He cares for you, He gives grace to the humble, He is the God of all grace, and on the promise of verse 10, which is pure gospel assurance underlying every command.

debates.question: How does this passage shape a right understanding of church leadership?

views.label: Servant shepherding, never domination
views.case: Elders lead by tending and example, not by lording it over the flock, motivated willingly rather than by compulsion or greed. Authority is real but exercised as humble, accountable care under the Chief Shepherd, modeled on Christ Himself.

views.label: Accountable stewardship of Christ's flock
views.case: Others stress that the flock is God's, not the elders', and that the Chief Shepherd will assess their work. Leadership is a trust to be discharged faithfully, with reward or loss depending on how the sheep have been cared for.

debates.bearing: Both readings define godly leadership as humble, willing, exemplary care of a flock that belongs to Christ, exercised under His authority and answerable to Him at His appearing.

debates.question: How real and how dangerous is the devil in verse 8?

views.label: A real, dangerous, but defeated enemy
views.case: The devil truly prowls to devour, so believers must be sober and alert. Yet he is resisted by standing firm in faith, not by fear, for the God of all grace will soon restore His people. The lion roars, but he is on a leash under God's power.

views.label: Chiefly the pressure of persecution and temptation
views.case: Some see the devil's prowling embodied especially in the persecution and temptations to abandon faith that the churches faced. Even so, the enemy behind these pressures is personal and real, and the response is the same: firm, faith-filled resistance.

debates.bearing: Both take the enemy seriously without granting him ultimate power. He is to be resisted by faith, in solidarity with the worldwide church, in confidence that God will have the final word.

study.originalLanguages: Peter, a fellow elder, charges the elders to poimaino, to shepherd, God's flock, tending it willingly and eagerly, never domineering, awaiting the archipoimen, the Chief Shepherd, who will give an unfading crown. All are to clothe themselves in tapeinophrosyne, humility, since God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, and so to humble themselves under God's mighty hand and cast their merimna, their anxiety, on Him who cares for them. They must stay alert against the antidikos, the adversary, the devil who prowls like a lion, resisting him firm in faith alongside the worldwide church. And the promise crowns it: the theos pases charitos, the God of all grace, will Himself katartizo, restore, mend, and make complete His people after a little suffering, securing, strengthening, and establishing them for eternal glory.

typology.type: Elders as under-shepherds of the flock
typology.fulfillment: The biblical shepherd theme from Psalm 23 and Ezekiel 34 through Jesus the Good Shepherd, now entrusted to elders who serve under Christ the Chief Shepherd.
The lasting reward that answers the perishable crowns of this world, given by the Chief Shepherd at His appearing.

typology.type: God's mighty hand that humbles and exalts
typology.fulfillment: The hand that delivered Israel from Egypt, now lifting the humble in due time and restoring His suffering people.

study.christ: Christ is the Chief Shepherd, the true owner of the flock under whom all elders serve as under-shepherds accountable to Him. Peter appeals as a witness of Christ's sufferings and a partaker of the glory to be revealed, so his charge is rooted in Christ's own path from suffering to glory. It is in Christ that God has called His people to eternal glory, and it is at Christ's appearing that the unfading crown is given. The whole closing charge leans toward the day the Chief Shepherd appears to gather and reward His flock.
study.god: God is revealed as gracious, caring, mighty, and faithful. He opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, and His mighty hand both humbles His people and, in due time, exalts them. He cares for them so personally that they can throw all their anxiety onto Him. Supremely He is the God of all grace, who after a little suffering will Himself restore, secure, strengthen, and establish His people, having called them to His eternal glory in Christ. To Him belongs the power forever and ever.

crossReferences.note: God against false shepherds and Himself the true Shepherd of His flock, the background to shepherding under the Chief Shepherd.
Jesus charges Peter to feed and tend His sheep, the commission Peter now passes to the elders.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble; humble yourselves and He will exalt you, quoted and echoed in verses 5 to 6.
Cast your burden on the LORD and He will sustain you, the promise behind casting all anxiety on God who cares.

study.oneStory: From the beginning God has shepherded His people, raising under-shepherds, judging false ones, and at last coming Himself in Christ, the Good and Chief Shepherd. The suffering church is His flock, guarded through a little while of affliction and headed for eternal glory. Clothed in humility, casting their cares on Him, and resisting the roaring enemy, believers rest in the God of all grace, who will Himself mend and establish them and bring them home. To that God, who carries the story to its end, belongs the power forever and ever.

quiz.q: How are elders to shepherd God's flock?

options.text: Willingly and eagerly, not for gain, and not lording it over the flock but as examples.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3: humble, willing, exemplary care, not domination or greed.

options.text: By exercising firm control so the people obey.
options.note: Peter forbids lording it over those entrusted to them.

options.text: Only if they are paid well for their trouble.
options.note: They are to serve not out of greed but eagerly.

options.text: By leading through humble example rather than compulsion.
options.note: True and close, though the answer should include willingly and not for gain.

quiz.q: What will faithful elders receive when the Chief Shepherd appears?

options.text: The crown of glory that will never fade away.
options.note: Verse 4: an unfading crown from Christ the Chief Shepherd.

options.text: Public honor and status in this life.
options.note: The reward is an eternal, unfading crown, not present status.

options.text: Nothing; faithful service is its own reward alone.
options.note: Peter promises a real reward at Christ's appearing.

options.text: An eternal reward given by Christ Himself.
options.note: True, though the answer should name the unfading crown of glory.

quiz.q: Why are believers to clothe themselves with humility?

options.text: Because God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
options.note: Verse 5: humility is the posture God blesses with grace.

options.text: Because humility makes people like them more.
options.note: The reason given is God's response to humility, not human approval.

options.text: Because they have nothing worth being proud of.
options.note: The ground is God's grace to the humble, not mere self-deprecation.

options.text: So that under God's mighty hand He may exalt them in due time.
options.note: True, verse 6, though the stated reason is that God gives grace to the humble.

quiz.q: How are believers to handle their anxieties?

options.text: Cast them all on God, because He cares for them.
options.note: Verse 7: throw the whole burden on God, grounded in His personal care.

options.text: Carry them privately so as not to burden God.
options.note: Peter says to cast them on God, who cares for you.

options.text: Ignore them and hope they pass.
options.note: The call is to entrust cares to God, not to ignore them.

options.text: Entrust every worry to the God who cares for them.
options.note: True to the sense, though the answer should stress casting them all on Him.

quiz.q: What does Peter promise from the God of all grace after a little suffering?

options.text: That God Himself will restore, secure, strengthen, and establish His people.
options.note: Verse 10: the letter's summit promise of God mending His suffering people.

options.text: That believers will be spared all further hardship at once.
options.note: The suffering is for a little while first; then God restores.

options.text: That the strong will make themselves secure.
options.note: It is God who Himself restores and establishes, not human strength.

options.text: That God will bring His people through suffering to eternal glory.
options.note: True to the sense, though the answer should name that God Himself restores and establishes them.

questions: Peter charges shepherds to lead humbly by example, not by domination. Wherever you have influence over others, how does this reshape the way you lead?
God gives grace to the humble but opposes the proud. Where is pride quietly at work in you, and what would it look like to clothe yourself in humility instead?
Cast all your anxiety on Him, because He cares for you. What specific worry will you hand to God today, trusting that He genuinely cares?
The devil prowls like a roaring lion, yet is to be resisted by faith. What lies or pressures are you facing, and how does standing firm in faith answer them?
The God of all grace promises to restore you after a little suffering. How does knowing your suffering is brief and your glory eternal steady you right now?

translationNotes.note: Be shepherds renders poimaino, to tend a flock; some manuscripts omit watching over them, but the sense of oversight remains in the surrounding phrases.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble quotes Proverbs 3:34 in its Greek form, also cited in James 4:6.
Restore translates katartizo, to mend or make complete, used of setting bones or repairing nets; some manuscripts add in Christ Jesus to called you to His eternal glory.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Peter 5:1-11. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
